Biblical Problem / Historical Issue: Historicity of the Patriarchs (Abraham, Isaac, Jacob)
Scholarly Debate: No direct archaeological evidence; anachronisms (camels, Philistines)
Evidence & Sources: Genesis 12–50; Mari tablets; Ur III period
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Patriarchal narratives reflect oral traditions of early 2nd millennium BCE seminomadic clans, later edited in Iron Age. Anachronisms may reflect updates.

Scholarly Debate: No Egyptian record of Hebrew slaves or exodus; Merneptah Stele mentions "Israel"
Evidence & Sources: Exodus 1–15; Papyrus Anastasi I; Avaris (Hyksos capital)
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Exodus may reflect memories of Hyksos expulsion or smaller-scale escapes, mythologized over time.

Scholarly Debate: Jericho’s walls fell c. 1550 BCE; Ai in ruins by 1200 BCE
Evidence & Sources: Joshua 6–8; archaeological layers; Hazael’s destruction of Gath
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Conquest narratives conflate memories of Bronze Age collapses, local conflicts, and later Davidic expansions.

Scholarly Debate: Minimal 10th-cent. BCE archaeology in Jerusalem; Tel Dan Stele confirms dynasty
Evidence & Sources: 1 Kings 1–11; Tel Dan Stele; Solomon’s gates at Hazor/Megiddo/Gezer
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: David/Solomon ruled a small kingdom; later scribes exaggerated their wealth and grandeur.

Scholarly Debate: Deuteronomy’s language matches 7th-cent. BCE; Genesis parallels Mesopotamian myths
Evidence & Sources: Deuteronomic Code; Enuma Elish; Covenant Code
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Torah compiled from older oral/written sources (J/E/D/P) during Babylonian exile.

Scholarly Debate: No mass graves; some life continued; many Jews stayed in Babylon
Evidence & Sources: Jeremiah 39; Ezra 1; Lachish Letters; Al-Yahudu tablets
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Exile was elite deportation (not total); diaspora Judaism emerged from cultural adaptation.

Scholarly Debate: No contemporary Roman/Jewish records; Paul’s letters lack details of empty tomb
Evidence & Sources: Gospels; Josephus (Testimonium Flavianum); 1 Cor 15:3–8
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Resurrection belief arose from visionary experiences, later narrative embellishment.

Scholarly Debate: Traditional Sinai location lacks evidence; alternative sites proposed
Evidence & Sources: Exodus 19; Egyptian mining records
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Sinai was a symbolic "mountain of God"; exact location lost.

Scholarly Debate: Philistines arrived c. 1175 BCE (after Patriarchal era); Genesis calls them "Philistines"
Evidence & Sources: Genesis 21; Ramses III inscriptions; Philistine pottery
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Anachronistic term for earlier Aegean traders or later editors updating names.

Scholarly Debate: Quirinius governor in 6 CE (after Herod’s death in 4 BCE); Jesus likely born before 4 BCE
Evidence & Sources: Luke 2; Josephus (Antiquities 18.1–2)
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Luke conflated a later census with Jesus’ birth narrative for theological symbolism.

Scholarly Debate: Widespread denial of historicity; implications for doctrine of sin
Evidence & Sources: Genesis 2–3; Paul’s letters; Second Temple literature
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Adamic narrative as archetype/myth; theological truth over literal history.

Scholarly Debate: Many books are pseudonymous; doubts over Mosaic authorship of Pentateuch, Pauline authorship of some letters
Evidence & Sources: Pentateuch; Isaiah; Pauline Epistles; historical-critical analysis
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Recognize literary conventions of pseudepigraphy; value theological message over strict authorship.

Scholarly Debate: Gospels shaped by faith communities; lack of non-Christian contemporary sources
Evidence & Sources: Synoptic Gospels; Josephus; Tacitus
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Historical Jesus reconstructed via critical methods (criteria of authenticity); full certainty elusive.

Scholarly Debate: Modern skepticism; lack of corroborating evidence
Evidence & Sources: Gospels; Acts; Josephus; Greco-Roman miracle accounts
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Miracles interpreted as theological narratives or symbolic acts, not empirical history.

Scholarly Debate: Absent from earliest Christian writings; parallels in Greco-Roman myths
Evidence & Sources: Matthew, Luke; Isaiah 7:14 LXX; Greco-Roman sources
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Virgin birth as theological motif expressing Jesus’ divine status; historicity debated.

Scholarly Debate: Ethical problem of divinely commanded violence; archaeological ambiguity
Evidence & Sources: Joshua; Judges; archaeological surveys; ANE conquest texts
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Narratives reflect ancient warfare rhetoric; some see as theological allegory or hyperbole.

Scholarly Debate: Patriarchal bias in biblical texts; limited roles for women
Evidence & Sources: Genesis 2–3; Pauline letters; Ruth; Esther
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Feminist and critical readings highlight counter-narratives and marginalized voices.

Scholarly Debate: Endorsement or regulation of slavery; NT household codes
Evidence & Sources: Exodus 21; Leviticus 25; Pauline letters
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Contextualize as ancient institution; modern readings emphasize trajectory toward liberation and equality.

Scholarly Debate: Contradictions between Genesis 1 and 2; parallels to other ANE creation myths
Evidence & Sources: Genesis 1–2; Enuma Elish; Atrahasis Epic
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Multiple sources combined; theological focus on God as creator, not scientific account.

Biblical Problem / Historical Issue: The Problem of Evil and Suffering
Scholarly Debate: Theodicy: reconciling God’s goodness with evil; Job as test case
Evidence & Sources: Job; Psalms; Ecclesiastes; Lamentations
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Diverse biblical responses: protest, trust, mystery; critical theory explores power and suffering.

Scholarly Debate: Historical context vs. predictive prophecy; coded language for oppressed communities
Evidence & Sources: Daniel; Revelation; intertestamental literature
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Apocalyptic texts as resistance literature; symbolic critique of empire and power.

Scholarly Debate: Disputed books; criteria for inclusion/exclusion; influence of church politics
Evidence & Sources: Early church councils; Dead Sea Scrolls; patristic writings
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Canon shaped by theological, liturgical, and political factors; critical theory highlights power dynamics.

Scholarly Debate: Thousands of manuscript variants; no original autographs
Evidence & Sources: Dead Sea Scrolls; Septuagint; Masoretic Text; NT papyri
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Textual criticism reconstructs earliest attainable text; recognizes fluidity and development of scripture.

Scholarly Debate: Evolving concepts in OT and NT; Sheol vs. resurrection; Greco-Roman influences
Evidence & Sources: Daniel 12; 1 Corinthians 15; 1 Enoch; Greco-Roman texts
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Development from shadowy afterlife to bodily resurrection; influenced by intercultural exchange.

Scholarly Debate: Israel’s election vs. universal salvation; ethnic boundaries in OT and NT
Evidence & Sources: Genesis 12; Isaiah 56; Acts 10; Galatians 3
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Critical theory interrogates exclusion/inclusion; NT moves toward universalism in Christ.

Scholarly Debate: Prophetic critique of inequality; NT communalism vs. later church practice
Evidence & Sources: Amos; Micah; Acts 2–4; James
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Emphasis on justice, care for poor; critical theory highlights economic power and systemic injustice.

Scholarly Debate: Is the Bible a reliable historical source or largely a literary/theological construct?
Evidence & Sources: Archaeological data; biblical texts; comparative ANE sources
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Most scholars now adopt a "moderate" approach, weighing literary, archaeological, and sociological data45.

Scholarly Debate: How much of the Bible is shaped by later editors/redactors?
Evidence & Sources: Source criticism, form criticism, redaction criticism
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Recognition of layered composition; focus on editorial motives and community contexts7.

Scholarly Debate: Archaeological findings often contradict or fail to confirm biblical narratives
Evidence & Sources: Excavation reports; surveys; biblical accounts
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Dialogue between archaeology and text; neither is privileged absolutely; both are interpreted in context34.

Scholarly Debate: How do power, empire, and colonialism shape biblical interpretation?
Evidence & Sources: Critical theory, postcolonial studies, liberation theology
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Emphasize voices of the marginalized, critique of empire, and the politics of interpretation.

Scholarly Debate: How have communities interpreted texts differently over time?
Evidence & Sources: Reception history, patristic writings, rabbinic literature
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Focus on meaning as constructed in interpretive communities, not just original context.

Biblical Problem / Historical Issue: Science and Evolution vs. Creation Narratives
Scholarly Debate: How do Genesis and science relate?
Evidence & Sources: Genesis 1–2; scientific evidence (cosmology, evolution)
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Many scholars see Genesis as theological, not scientific, text; others seek harmonization or reject one for the other3.

Biblical Problem / Historical Issue: The Role of Faith in Critical Scholarship
Scholarly Debate: Can faith-based and critical approaches be reconciled?
Evidence & Sources: Theological writings; historical-critical scholarship; church documents
Proposed Solution / Scholarly Trend: Ongoing debate; some advocate integration, others maintain strict separation7.

Scholarly Debate: All four Gospels record Judas’ agreement to betray Jesus for thirty pieces of silver (Matt 26:14-16; Mark 14:10-11; Luke 22:3-6; John 13:2, 27).
At the Last Supper, Jesus identifies Judas as the betrayer (Matt 26:21-25; Mark 14:18-21; Luke 22:21-23; John 13:21-30).
Matthew 27:3-10: Judas, filled with remorse, returns the silver to the temple, hangs himself; priests buy the “Field of Blood.”
Acts 1:16-20: Judas uses the money to buy a field, falls headlong, his body bursts open. The field is called Akeldama.
The two accounts are often harmonized as Judas hanging himself and, after death, his body falling (possibly due to decomposition or being cut down), resulting in the described injuries.
Luke uniquely includes Jesus being sent to Herod Antipas (Luke 23:6-12)1.
John emphasizes Jesus’ private dialogue with Pilate and omits the Sanhedrin trial details found in Synoptics.
Matthew includes Pilate’s wife’s dream and Pilate washing his hands (Matt 27:19, 24).
The Gospels vary in the words spoken from the cross, the presence of the two thieves, and the reactions of bystanders.
John places Jesus’ death before the Passover meal; Synoptics place it after.
